title: Analytics Platform Cost Comparison for 2026
description: How much does web and product analytics cost in 2026? Compare pricing models across Google Analytics, Mixpanel, Amplitude, Hotjar, Plausible, and Fathom — covering free tiers, event-based pricing, and pageview-based pricing.
body:
Analytics platform pricing follows fundamentally different models depending on whether the tool tracks pageviews (web analytics), events (product analytics), or user sessions (session recording and heatmaps). The cost of analytics scales with data volume — more pageviews, events, or recorded sessions increase the subscription cost — creating a variable expense that grows with the application's user base.

This comparison examines the pricing models across web analytics, product analytics, and user behavior analytics tools. All pricing is based on published rates at the time of writing. Check each vendor's current pricing page for exact figures.

## Google Analytics

Google Analytics provides a free tier that covers the majority of website analytics needs — pageview tracking, user demographics, acquisition source analysis, and goal conversion tracking. The free tier supports up to a substantial number of monthly hits with standard reporting features. Google Analytics 4 provides event-based tracking with cross-platform support for web and mobile applications.

Google Analytics' advantage is the free tier quality. For most small to medium websites, the free tier provides sufficient data collection, reporting, and integration capabilities without any subscription cost. The integration with Google Ads, Google Search Console, and Google BigQuery provides a connected marketing analytics ecosystem.

The limitation is the data sampling on higher-traffic properties and the complexity of GA4's event-based model compared to the previous Universal Analytics interface.

## Mixpanel

Mixpanel provides product analytics with event tracking, user segmentation, funnel analysis, and retention reporting. The Free tier supports a limited number of tracked users and data points per month. Paid tiers increase event volume limits, add advanced analysis capabilities, and provide team collaboration features.

Mixpanel's advantage is the event-based analysis depth. Product teams can track user interactions — button clicks, page views, feature usage — and analyze behavior across funnels, retention cohorts, and user segments without predefined reports.

The limitation is the per-event pricing. Applications with high event volumes face escalating costs, and teams must implement event prioritization and sampling to control spending.

Check Mixpanel's current pricing page for exact rates on Growth and Enterprise tiers and data volume limits.

## Amplitude

Amplitude provides product analytics with a focus on user behavior analysis, behavioral cohorts, and predictive analytics. The Free tier supports a limited number of monthly tracked users. Paid tiers increase user limits and add advanced analytics features.

Amplitude's advantage is the behavioral analytics depth. Amplitude's platform analyzes user behavior patterns — feature adoption, session frequency, conversion paths — and provides predictive models for churn risk and revenue forecasting.

The limitation mirrors Mixpanel's: event-based pricing that scales with tracking volume, requiring teams to manage data collection costs.

Check Amplitude's current pricing page for exact rates on Plus, Growth, and Enterprise tiers.

## Hotjar

Hotjar provides session recording, heatmaps, and user feedback tools that complement analytics platforms. Recordings capture individual user sessions for qualitative analysis. Heatmaps aggregate click, scroll, and mouse movement data across pages. Feedback widgets collect in-page user feedback.

Hotjar's advantage is the qualitative insight layer. Session recordings show what users actually do on the page, revealing usability issues that quantitative analytics tools cannot detect. Heatmaps identify which page elements get the most attention and interaction.

The limitation is the sample-based recording. Hotjar records a subset of sessions based on a configurable sampling rate, capturing trends rather than comprehensive data.

Check Hotjar's current pricing page for exact rates on Plus, Business, and Scale tiers with session recording limits.

## Plausible

Plausible provides lightweight, privacy-focused web analytics without cookies or personal data collection. The platform tracks pageviews, unique visitors, referrers, and goal conversions with a simple dashboard. Pricing is based on monthly pageview volume.

Plausible's advantage is the simplicity and privacy compliance. The GDPR-compliant tracking requires no cookie consent banner, and the dashboard shows essential metrics without overwhelming detail. The lightweight script does not affect page load performance.

The limitation is the analytics depth. Plausible provides aggregate pageview and visitor data but does not support event tracking, user segmentation, funnel analysis, or behavioral cohorts.

Check Plausible's current pricing page for exact rates based on monthly pageview volume.

## Fathom

Fathom provides privacy-focused web analytics with a similar approach to Plausible — cookie-free tracking, simple dashboards, and pageview-based pricing. Fathom offers a self-hosted version for teams that need complete data control.

Fathom's advantage is the self-hosting option. Teams that require complete control over analytics data can run Fathom on their own infrastructure with a one-time license fee, avoiding per-pageview subscription costs.

The limitation is the feature set — Fathom provides aggregate analytics without event tracking, user-level analysis, or advanced segmentation capabilities.

Check Fathom's current pricing page for exact rates on cloud and self-hosted plans.

## Pricing Model Comparison

The analytics pricing landscape divides into three models. Pageview-based pricing (Plausible, Fathom) scales with website traffic and is most predictable for content sites with steady traffic growth. Event-based pricing (Mixpanel, Amplitude) scales with tracking volume and requires teams to manage data collection costs through event prioritization. Feature-tier pricing (Google Analytics, Hotjar) provides fixed feature sets at each tier with limits on data volume or recorded sessions.

The right model depends on the type of analysis needed. Content sites that need pageview and visitor analytics benefit from pageview-based pricing. Product teams that need event-level user behavior analysis require event-based platforms. Teams combining quantitative and qualitative analytics need both an analytics platform and a session recording tool.
author: PilotStack Team
